glossary.def: For a discrete random variable, the rule p(x) giving the probability of each individual outcome, with all the masses summing to one. Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson and Geometric are all specified by a PMF.

For a continuous random variable, a curve f(x) whose area between two points equals the probability of landing in that range; the total area under it is one. No single point carries positive probability.

The result that the average of many independent draws from any distribution with finite mean and variance becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows — which is why the bell curve appears everywhere a quantity is a sum of small effects.
The standard deviation of a sample mean, equal to sigma/sqrt(n). It shrinks like 1/sqrt(n), so halving your uncertainty about an average requires quadrupling the sample.

The standardised fourth moment, measuring how much probability sits in the tails. The Normal has kurtosis 3, so practitioners report excess kurtosis (kurtosis minus 3): zero for a Normal, positive for heavy-tailed distributions that throw extreme values more often.
The property that elapsed waiting time gives no information about remaining waiting time. The Exponential is the only continuous distribution with it, and the Geometric the only discrete one.

A distribution whose probability of extreme values decays far more slowly than a Normal's — polynomially rather than exponentially. Financial returns are heavy-tailed, so Normal-based risk models systematically under-price catastrophic moves.

A symmetric bell that matches the Normal in the centre but has heavier, polynomially-decaying tails controlled by the degrees of freedom nu; small nu means fat tails and it converges to the Normal as nu grows. The standard model for daily and weekly asset returns.

refs.cite: Wasserman, L. (2004). All of Statistics: A Concise Course in Statistical Inference. Springer.
Fischer, H. (2011). A History of the Central Limit Theorem: From Classical to Modern Probability Theory. Springer.
Student [Gosset, W. S.] (1908). The Probable Error of a Mean. Biometrika 6(1), 1-25.
Mandelbrot, B. (1963). The Variation of Certain Speculative Prices. Journal of Business 36(4), 394-419.
Cont, R. (2001). Empirical Properties of Asset Returns: Stylized Facts and Statistical Issues. Quantitative Finance 1(2), 223-236.
Clauset, A., Shalizi, C. R. & Newman, M. E. J. (2009). Power-Law Distributions in Empirical Data. SIAM Review 51(4), 661-703.
